Checking sign-in…

Accompaniment Styles

These patterns are built into the accompaniment engine and can be selected on any tune page. Each style adapts automatically to the tune's meter and chord chart. Styles marked any meter work in 2/4, 3/4, 4/4, and 6/8; fixed-meter styles fall back to block chords when the meter doesn't match. Examples shown in C major at 110 bpm.

Jazz
Jazz Charleston charleston 4/4

Chord on beat 1 and the and-of-2, leaving beats 3 and 4 open. Named for the 1920s dance; the defining syncopation of early jazz and Swing Era big-band writing.

Jazz Swing Piano Full band
HistoryComing soon
ListeningComing soon
Jazz waltz jazzwaltz 3/4

Swung bass on beat 1, ride-cymbal implied chord on beat 3. The feel of bebop and post-bop piano trios — Evans, Peterson, Monk in 3/4.

Jazz Bebop Piano trio
HistoryComing soon
ListeningComing soon
Latin
Bossa nova bossa 4/4

Tresillo-derived bass pattern with chords on the off-beats; ride cymbal and side-stick percussion. Rooted in the guitar style of João Gilberto and the samba-jazz fusion of late-1950s Rio.

Latin Jazz Brazilian Guitar Piano
HistoryComing soon
ListeningComing soon
Tango tango 4/4

Habanera rhythmic cell in the bass (long–short–short–long) with syncopated chords. Descended from Cuban contradanza via the Argentine arrabal — the tresillo pattern that underlies both the habanera and early tango.

Latin Argentine Cuban Bandoneón Piano
HistoryComing soon
ListeningComing soon
Waltz & Dance
Waltz waltz 3/4

Bass on beat 1, chord on beats 2 and 3. The universal dance-band formula for 3/4 time. The slight delay before the chord creates the characteristic lilt that distinguishes waltz from a march.

Dance Classical Piano Orchestra
HistoryComing soon
ListeningComing soon
Viennese waltz viennese 3/4

A short downbeat chord followed by slightly-early beats 2 and 3 (the "anticipated" Viennese lilt). The elastic timing distinguishes the Strauss concert hall waltz from the plainer ballroom waltz.

Dance Classical Romantic Orchestra Piano
HistoryComing soon
ListeningComing soon
Boom-chick oompah any

Root on the downbeat, chord on every other beat. The engine of polka, march, and German dance-band styles; derived from tuba–horn interplay in brass ensembles.

Folk Polka March Brass Accordion
HistoryComing soon
ListeningComing soon
Folk & Country
Folk — alternating bass folk any

Alternating root–fifth bass note with a chord on the off-beat. The defining guitar and banjo accompaniment pattern in American folk, bluegrass, and country; also called the "Carter scratch."

Folk Country Bluegrass Guitar Banjo
HistoryComing soon
ListeningComing soon
Pop & Rock
Pop / ballad pop any

Bass on beats 1 and 3, steady quarter-note chords on all beats. The most common piano and keyboard pattern in popular music since the 1950s.

Pop Rock Piano Keyboard
HistoryComing soon
ListeningComing soon
Driving 8ths driving8 any

Steady eighth-note chords with no gaps. Propulsive and energetic; fills every subdivision. Found across rock, country, and pop guitar — sometimes called a "strum pattern."

Rock Country Pop Guitar Piano
HistoryComing soon
ListeningComing soon
Offbeat chords offbeat any

Chord only on the off-beats, silence on the downbeats. The defining rhythmic gesture of ska and reggae; known as the "skank" or "chop." In reggae, the kick lands on beat 3 (the "one-drop").

Ska Reggae Guitar Keyboard
HistoryComing soon
ListeningComing soon
Classical & Piano
Block chords block any

All voices together on every beat, sustained until the next chord. The simplest possible accompaniment; used for chorale writing, teaching chord shapes, and very slow tempos.

Classical Chorale Piano Keyboard
HistoryComing soon
ListeningComing soon
Piano arpeggio ↑ arpflow 4/4

Rising arpeggios through the full bar (root–third–fifth–octave). Characteristic of Romantic-era piano writing and nocturne accompaniment; adds forward motion when chord changes are sparse.

Classical Romantic Piano
HistoryComing soon
ListeningComing soon
Arpeggio (root–5th) arpcell any

Root and fifth as individual tones in a repeating per-beat cell. Creates a light, open texture that suggests harmonic motion without dense block chords; useful for harp or guitar figuration.

Classical Pop Harp Guitar Piano
HistoryComing soon
ListeningComing soon
Bass line (roots) bassroot any

Root note only in the bass, no chord voices above. Use as a harmonic foundation when the melody instrument carries the full harmonic content, or when learning to hear bass motion in isolation.

Teaching Minimal Bass
HistoryComing soon
ListeningComing soon
Piano & Keyboard
Block chordsblockany

Sustained block voicing on every beat. The starting point for learning chord shapes and for chorale-style writing.

ClassicalChorale
Waltzwaltz3/4

Left-hand bass on 1, right-hand chord on 2–3. The standard piano waltz texture from Chopin through the ballroom repertoire.

DanceClassical
Pop / balladpopany

Bass on 1 and 3, chords on every beat. Ubiquitous in pop piano; easy to adapt to either hands-together or stride-style playing.

PopRock
Piano arpeggio ↑arpflow4/4

Rising broken chord through the full bar. Nocturne and ballad texture; standard left-hand figure in Romantic piano writing.

ClassicalRomantic
Viennese waltzviennese3/4

Elastic 3/4 with the characteristic early-beat anticipation. Strauss orchestra and salon piano repertoire.

DanceClassical
Jazz Charlestoncharleston4/4

Syncopated 4/4 comping with a chord on beat 1 and the and-of-2. Common in Stride, Swing, and mainstream jazz piano.

JazzSwing
Jazz waltzjazzwaltz3/4

Swung bass on 1, light chord on 3. The texture of jazz piano trios playing in 3 — Evans, Peterson, Monk.

JazzBebop
Guitar & Plucked Strings
Folk — alternating bassfolkany

Thumb alternates root–fifth in the bass; fingers strum the chord on the off-beat. The foundational fingerpicking and flatpicking pattern in folk, bluegrass, and country guitar.

FolkCountryBluegrass
Driving 8thsdriving8any

Steady downstroke eighth notes, no rests. The strum pattern behind countless rock, country, and pop songs.

RockCountryPop
Offbeat chordsoffbeatany

The ska/reggae "skank": chord only between the beats. In reggae the downstroke is muted, leaving only the upstroke chop.

SkaReggae
Bossa novabossa4/4

Tresillo bass with off-beat chords. João Gilberto's original bossa guitar part is the blueprint; the pattern is also widely adapted to keyboard.

LatinBrazilianJazz
Arpeggio (root–5th)arpcellany

Open-voiced ascending tones per beat. Evokes harp, classical guitar, and lute figuration; widely used in Renaissance and Baroque lute tablature.

ClassicalPop
Brass & Ensemble
Boom-chickoompahany

Tuba carries the root on 1; horns answer with the chord on 2 (and 4). The pattern maps directly to the brass section interplay in polka bands, march bands, and German Blasmusik.

PolkaMarchFolk
Tangotango4/4

Habanera cell in the bass with syncopated upper chords. In Argentine tango this interplay moves between bandoneón and piano; in orchestral tango (Piazzolla) it drives the entire ensemble.

LatinArgentine
Bass & Foundation
Bass line (roots)bassrootany

Root note only, no chords. Use this when practicing bass lines in isolation, or when the melody is dense enough to imply the harmony on its own.

TeachingMinimal
